name: Atalanta
description: Atalanta, a legendary huntress in Greek mythology, was abandoned as an infant by her father, Iasus, who desired a son. She was raised by a she-bear (in some accounts) or hunters, developing exceptional skills in hunting and wilderness survival. Atalanta vowed to remain a virgin and refused to marry, but her beauty attracted many suitors. She challenged them to a footrace, promising to marry the victor but killing those who lost. Many suitors perished until Hippomenes, aided by Aphrodite, used golden apples to distract Atalanta during the race. She stopped to pick up the apples, allowing Hippomenes to win. They married, but later angered either Zeus or Cybele, and were transformed into lions. Atalanta is celebrated for her independence, athletic prowess, and unwavering determination. She participated in the Calydonian Boar Hunt, where she was the first to wound the beast, earning praise from Meleager. Her story explores themes of gender roles, fate, and the consequences of defying the gods. She is a symbol of female empowerment and a reminder of the strength and skill that can be found in those who challenge societal expectations. Her tale is a complex blend of triumph and tragedy, reflecting the often-unpredictable nature of Greek mythology.
subtitle: Swift-footed huntress of Greek legend
achievements: First to wound the Calydonian Boar
Defeated many suitors in a footrace
Member of the Argonauts (in some accounts)

keyMyths.title: The Calydonian Boar Hunt
keyMyths.description: Atalanta joined the hunt for the Calydonian Boar, a fearsome beast sent by Artemis. She was the first to wound the boar, earning praise and recognition for her skill.

keyMyths.title: The Footrace for Marriage
keyMyths.description: To avoid marriage, Atalanta challenged her suitors to a footrace, killing those who lost. Hippomenes, with Aphrodite's help, defeated her using golden apples.

keyMyths.title: Transformation into Lions
keyMyths.description: Atalanta and Hippomenes were transformed into lions for either disrespecting Zeus or Cybele. They were condemned to forever pull Cybele's chariot.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A Roman narrative poem that includes Atalanta's stories, particularly the Calydonian Boar hunt and the footrace.
sources.text: A Roman narrative poem that includes Atalanta's stories, particularly the Calydonian Boar hunt and the footrace.

sources.author: Apollonius Rhodius
sources.description: An epic poem that includes Atalanta as a member of the Argonauts (in some versions).
sources.text: An epic poem that includes Atalanta as a member of the Argonauts (in some versions).
